content:
Hello everyone,

I recently started developing for the kernel, as part of my research project on the usability of Rust for the develompent of Kernel modules for distributed systems.
For this reason, I noticed the lack of the "net" module in the rust-dev branch. I read on the patch archive that there is a work in progress on the net module for Ethernet binding.
I am interested in contributing for the net module development, mainly on bringing the socket interface to the Rust API.
Is it something already being worked on? How could I contribute?

Apologies if this is not the right communication channel for such a request; mailing lists are something new to me. Let me know if I should write there instead.
If so, it seems like they are looking for real projects (drivers suitable for mainline kernel) that could be picked up. Do you have anything specific in mind?
@FUJITA Tomonori might be able to say more about that whenever they're back online

In the meanwhile, you could open an issue like https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/1004 for a clear picture of what you plan to do and the dependencies. It'll be easier for people to understand whether what they are working has the same dependencies, and you can start from there.
Sounds good, I will start working on that then. Thanks for the advice.

@Michele Dalle Rive

There is a bindings for basic operations for socket in the rust branch:
https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/blob/rust/rust/kernel/net.rs

Or you are looking for a bindings for accessing to the internal of socket like sk_data_ready hook?

Can you share the details of your requirements?

I saw that module and I have been using it recently to get started with networking. However, I specifically need to handle UDP sockets: since there is no generic socket struct in the Rust module and no UDP-specific structure (like the TcpListener in that module), I thought it would be a good idea to contribute to the rust-dev branch with a more complete net module, maybe using the one in the 'rust' branch as a starting point, why not.

My main requirements are just being able to create udp sockets and use their basic operations, for now; also, being able to manage everything related to the socket, which is handling the addresses, protocols, socket options etc, without having to use the C bindings in the module code (which should be avoided).
I will explain everything thoroughly and precisely in the GitHub issue, as suggested by @Boqun Feng. I will link it here once I am done.
Hopefully it can have a positive impact in the networking module development.

I posted a bindings for operations for generic socket (works for UDP/TCP), which you might be interested in:
https://github.com/fujita/linux/blob/rust-tls/rust/kernel/net.rs

Looks interesting. Are you still actively developing it?
I was planning to implement a similar generic Socket, to provide a C-like generic interface. The problem I noticed with it is that it requires a large abstraction: for example, in the sendmsg function, the caller must provide an instance of some abstraction of msghdr because, for UDP sockets, it must contain the sockaddr struct of the receiver of the packet. In the same way, when receiving a message, the UDP socket must return the sender address, while a TCP socket does not really need it (?)

I suspect that there is little chance of upstreaming the abstractions for now so I focus on bindings for network device drivers.
Feel free to work on the top of it.
This topic was moved here from #Library > Networking core by Boqun Feng.
Hi everyone, I opened an issue (https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/linux/issues/1022) explaining my idea for the Rust networking socket binding. Lemme know if you have any suggestion or comment :)
